query: What are the complete daily nutritional requirements for 4 astronauts on Mars? Include calories, protein, carbohydrates, fat, vitamins (A, C, D, K, B-complex), and minerals (iron, calcium, potassium, zinc). Break down by activity level.

content.text: {"statusCode":200,"body":"{\"retrieved_chunks\": [{\"content\": \"# 5. Human Nutritional Strategy for a 4-Astronaut Mars Surface Mission\\n## 5.1 Purpose\\nThis document defines the nutritional requirements of a 4-astronaut crew during a 450-day Mars surface mission and establishes a framework for optimizing greenhouse crop production to supplement stored food supplies.\\nThe objective is to:\\n* Maximize nutritional coverage\\n* Maintain physiological health in reduced gravity\\n* Support cognitive performance\\n* Minimize reliance on pre-packaged food\\n* Optimize resource use (water, energy, area)\\nThis framework is designed to support AI agents in making crop allocation and environmental control decisions.\\n---\\n# 5.2 Daily Nutritional Requirements per Astronaut\\nValues represent average adult requirements under moderate physical activity.\\n## 5.2.1 Energy (Calories)\\n* 2,500 \\u2013 3,800 kcal/day per astronaut\\n* Mission planning average assumption: ~3,000 kcal/day\\nFor 4 astronauts:\\n* \\u2248 12,000 kcal/day total\\n* \\u2248 5.4 million kcal over 450 days\\nGreenhouse objective:\\nSupplement a significant fraction of total caloric intake (target variable for optimization).\\n---\\n## 5.2.2 Macronutrient Distribution\\nRecommended macronutrient breakdown:\\n* Carbohydrates: ~45\\u201355% of total calories\\n* Protein: ~15\\u201320% of total calories\\n* Fat: ~30\\u201335% of total calories\\nMars greenhouse is expected to primarily supply:\\n* Carbohydrates\\n* Plant-based protein\\n* Micronutrients\\nFat supply likely partially reliant on stored food unless oil crops are introduced.\\n---\\n## 5.2.3 Protein Requirements\\nRecommended intake:\\n* 1.2\\u20131.8 g protein per kg body mass\\n* Assume 75 kg astronaut average\\nProtein per astronaut:\\n~90\\u2013135 g/day\\nFor 4 astronauts:\\n~360\\u2013540 g protein/day\\nImportance in space:\\n* Muscle mass preservation in 0.38g gravity\\n* Immune function\\n* Tissue repair\\n---\\n## 5.2.4 Micronutrients\\nCritical micronutrients include:\\n* Vitamin A\\n* Vitamin C\\n* Vitamin K\\n* Folate\\n* Iron\\n* Potassium\\n* Magnesium\\nDeficiencies can impact:\\n* Bone health\\n* Immune function\\n* Cognitive performance\\n* Cardiovascular stability\\nLeafy greens are primary micronutrient suppliers.\\n---\\n## 5.2.5 Water\\nMinimum hydration requirement:\\n* 2.1\\u20132.5 L/day per astronaut\\nTotal crew:\\n~8\\u201310 L/day\\nWater for irrigation must be efficiently recycled and managed within the greenhouse system.\\n---\\n# 5.3 Nutritional Contribution by Crop Type\\n## 5.3.1 Caloric Contribution\\n| Crop | Approx kcal per 100g | Role |\\n| - | - | - |\\n| Potato | ~77 kcal | Primary energy |\\n| Beans/Peas | ~80\\u2013120 kcal | Protein + energy |\\n| Lettuce | ~15 kcal | Micronutrients |\\n| Radish | ~16 kcal | Diet diversity |\\nHigh-calorie density crops are necessary for meeting total energy demand.\\n---\\n## 5.3.2 Protein Contribution\\nPrimary greenhouse protein source:\\n* Beans and peas\\nSecondary contribution:\\n* Potatoes (low but additive)\\n* Leafy greens (minor contribution)\\nIf protein deficit occurs, agents should:\\n* Increase legume production\\n* Adjust nutrient solution to support pod development\\n---\\n## 5.3.3 Micronutrient Contribution\\nLeafy greens provide:\\n* Vitamin K\\n* Vitamin A\\n* Folate\\nRadishes contribute:\\n* Vitamin C\\nPotatoes provide:\\n* Potassium\\n* Some Vitamin C\\nBalanced crop diversity prevents micronutrient monotony.\\n---\\n# 5.4 Nutritional Optimization Logic\\nAI agents may implement dynamic nutritional balancing.\\n## 5.4.1 Caloric Deficit Scenario\\nIF:\\n* Total daily caloric production < 12,000 kcal\\nTHEN:\\n* Increase potato allocation\\n* Increase planting density of calorie-dense crops\\n* Adjust light intensity to boost tuber biomass\\n* Temporarily reduce low-calorie crops\\n---\\n## 5.4.2 Protein Deficit Scenario\\nIF:\\n* Daily protein production < 360 g\\nTHEN:\\n* Increase legume cultivation area\\n* Optimize flowering conditions\\n* Adjust nitrogen levels\\n* Extend legume harvest cycle\\n---\\n## 5.4.3 Micronutrient Risk Scenario\\nIF:\\n* Leafy green yield decreases\\n  OR\\n* Micronutrient coverage insufficient\\nTHEN:\\n* Prioritize fast-cycle leafy crops\\n* Increase nitrogen availability\\n* Optimize CO\\u2082 enrichment\\n---\\n# 5.5 Crop Allocation Strategy Model\\nGreenhouse area allocation must balance:\\n* Energy security\\n* Protein adequacy\\n* Dietary diversity\\n* Growth cycle stagger\\nExample strategic allocation model:\\n* 40\\u201350% area: Potatoes\\n* 20\\u201330% area: Legumes\\n* 15\\u201320% area: Leafy greens\\n* 5\\u201310% area: Radishes & herbs\\nAI agents may adjust allocation dynamically based on:\\n* Resource availability\\n* Stress events\\n* Energy constraints\\n* Nutritional deficits\\n---\\n# 5.6 Time-Scale Considerations\\nShort-cycle crops:\\n* Lettuce\\n* Radish\\nAdvantages:\\n* Rapid nutritional correction\\n* System feedback indicator\\nLong-cycle crops:\\n* Potatoes\\nAdvantages:\\n* High caloric return\\n* Energy backbone\\nBalanced portfolio prevents single-point failure.\\n---\\n# 5.7 Sustainability & Closed-Loop Considerations\\nNutritional optimization must integrate:\\n* Water recycling efficiency\\n* Nutrient recovery\\n* Crop residue composting (if applicable)\\n* Waste minimization\\nTrade-off:\\nHigher yield may require higher energy input.\\nAI agents must optimize:\\nYield per unit of energy and water.\\n---\\n# 5.8 Nutritional Risk Hierarchy\\nMission-critical priorities:\\n1. Total caloric sufficiency\\n2. Adequate protein supply\\n3. Micronutrient diversity\\n4. Psychological satisfaction\\nShort-term micronutrient variation is tolerable.\\nSustained caloric or protein deficit is not.\\n---\\n# 5.9 AI-Oriented Nutritional Control Framework\\nAutonomous greenhouse agents may follow:\\n1. Calculate daily nutrient output\\n2. Compare with crew requirements\\n3. Detect deficit or surplus\\n4. Adjust crop ratio or growth parameters\\n5. Monitor correction trajectory\\nExample:\\nIF:\\nCalorie output = 9,500 kcal/day\\nTarget = 12,000 kcal/day\\nGap = 2,500 kcal\\nSystem response:\\n* Increase potato planting density\\n* Increase photoperiod for tuber crops\\n* Reallocate low-calorie crops\\n---\\n# 5.10 Mission-Level Objective Function\\nA simplified optimization goal for agents:\\nMaximize:\\nNutritional Coverage Score\\nSubject to:\\n* Water constraint\\n* Energy constraint\\n* Area constraint\\n* System stability constraint\\nWhere:\\nNutritional Coverage Score =\\nWeighted coverage of calories + protein + micronutrients.\\n\", \"score\": 0.85294235, \"location\": {\"s3Location\": {\"uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/05_Human_Nutritional_Strategy.md\"}, \"type\": \"S3\"}, \"metadata\": {\"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/05_Human_Nutritional_Strategy.md\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-file-modality\": \"TEXT\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-data-source-id\": \"OGUYTEJRIW\"}}, {\"content\": \"# 1. Extended Mars Environmental and Physical Constraints\\n## 1.1 Purpose\\nThis document defines the environmental conditions of Mars relevant to controlled agriculture and autonomous greenhouse system design.\\nThe objective is to establish:\\n* Boundary conditions\\n* Physical constraints\\n* Operational implications\\n* AI-relevant parameters\\nThese factors determine system design requirements.\\n---\\n## 1.2 Atmospheric Composition and Pressure\\n### Composition\\nMartian atmosphere (approximate):\\n* Carbon Dioxide (CO\\u2082): 95.32%\\n* Nitrogen (N\\u2082): 2.7%\\n* Argon (Ar): 1.6%\\n* Oxygen (O\\u2082): 0.13%\\nImplications:\\n* High CO\\u2082 concentration (beneficial for photosynthesis)\\n* Extremely low oxygen availability (greenhouse must be sealed)\\n* Minimal atmospheric support for human life\\n---\\n### Pressure\\nAverage surface pressure:\\n* 6\\u20137 millibars\\n* <1% of Earth\\u2019s sea-level pressure (~1013 millibars)\\nImplications:\\n* Greenhouse must be pressurized\\n* Structural design must resist pressure differentials\\n* External environment cannot support liquid water\\n---\\n## 1.3 Temperature Profile\\nAverage temperature:\\n* ~ -63\\u00b0C\\n* Range: -140\\u00b0C to +21\\u00b0C (seasonal and regional variation)\\nImplications:\\n* External environment is inhospitable to plant life\\n* Greenhouse requires thermal insulation and active climate control\\n* Energy expenditure for heating is significant\\nAI agents must manage:\\n* Temperature regulation\\n* Energy trade-offs\\n* Crop sensitivity to thermal variation\\n---\\n## 1.4 Solar Irradiance and Lighting\\nSolar irradiance:\\n* ~590 W/m\\u00b2 at equator noon\\n* Approximately 43% of Earth\\u2019s peak irradiance\\nAdditional considerations:\\n* Atmospheric dust reduces light transmission\\n* Light is more diffuse than on Earth\\n* Seasonal and regional variation exists\\nImplications for agriculture:\\n* Artificial lighting is mandatory for reliable growth\\n* LED systems are preferred (efficiency and spectrum control)\\n* Photoperiod management becomes a control variable\\nTypical PAR (Photosynthetically Active Radiation):\\n* 400\\u2013700 nm spectrum\\n* Target intensity for leafy crops: 150\\u2013250 \\u00b5mol/m\\u00b2/s\\n---\\n## 1.5 Gravity\\nMartian gravity:\\n* 3.721 m/s\\u00b2\\n* ~38% of Earth gravity\\nImplications for plant biology:\\n* Altered water and nutrient transport\\n* Potential differences in stem strength and morphology\\n* Root-zone dynamics may vary\\nAI agents should monitor:\\n* Growth patterns\\n* Structural stability\\n* Resource distribution behavior\\n---\\n## 1.6 Regolith (Martian Soil)\\nComposition:\\n* Fine rocky particles\\n* Low organic content\\n* Contains toxic perchlorates\\nCharacteristics:\\n* Not naturally fertile\\n* Requires processing for agricultural use\\n* Potential chemical hazards\\nImplications:\\n* Direct soil cultivation is not viable\\n* Hydroponic or aeroponic systems are preferred\\n* If soil is used, detoxification and amendment are required\\nPerchlorates:\\n* Toxic to plants and humans\\n* Must be removed or neutralized\\n* Represent a significant design constraint\\n---\\n## 1.7 Radiation Environment\\nMars lacks a global magnetic field and thick atmosphere.\\nConsequences:\\n* Higher radiation exposure than Earth\\n* Potential risk to biological systems\\n* Greenhouse structures provide shielding\\nAgricultural implication:\\n* Internal greenhouse environment mitigates radiation\\n* Material selection may contribute to shielding\\n* Sensor and electronic systems require protection\\n---\\n## 1.8 Water Availability\\nEvidence suggests:\\n* Water ice exists in subsurface deposits\\n* Liquid water is unstable on the surface\\n* Extraction and processing would be required\\nImplications:\\n* Water is a precious resource\\n* Closed-loop recycling is mandatory\\n* Efficient irrigation strategies are essential\\nAI agents should optimize:\\n* Water use\\n* Recycling efficiency\\n* Crop selection for water productivity\\n---\\n## 1.9 Environmental Constraints Summary\\n| Parameter | Value | Implication |\\n| - | - | - |\\n| Pressure | 6\\u20137 mbar | Greenhouse must be sealed |\\n| Temperature | -140\\u00b0C to +21\\u00b0C | Active climate control required |\\n| Irradiance | ~43% of Earth | Artificial lighting needed |\\n| Gravity | 38% of Earth | Biological effects expected |\\n| Soil | Perchlorate presence | Hydroponics preferred |\\n| Atmosphere | CO\\u2082 rich, O\\u2082 poor | Sealed environment required |\\n---\\n## 1.10 AI-Relevant Considerations\\nAutonomous systems must account for:\\n* Environmental variability\\n* Resource constraints\\n* System feedback loops\\n* Multi-variable optimization\\nDecision logic should be data-driven and adaptive.\\n---\\n## 1.11 Scientific Rigor and Assumptions\\nValues provided represent typical or average conditions.\\nActual mission conditions may vary.\\nAI agents should:\\n* Validate sensor data\\n* Handle uncertainty\\n* Adapt to observed conditions\\n\", \"score\": 0.43122646, \"location\": {\"s3Location\": {\"uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/01_Mars_Environment_Extended.md\"}, \"type\": \"S3\"}, \"metadata\": {\"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/01_Mars_Environment_Extended.md\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-file-modality\": \"TEXT\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-data-source-id\": \"OGUYTEJRIW\"}}, {\"content\": \"Excellent choice.\\n# 3. Extended Crop Profiles for Martian Controlled Environment Agriculture\\n## 3.1 Purpose\\nThis document provides structured agronomic profiles of selected crops suitable for a Martian greenhouse supporting a crew of four astronauts during a 450-day surface mission.\\nEach crop profile includes:\\n* Growth characteristics\\n* Yield potential\\n* Resource requirements\\n* Nutritional contribution\\n* Environmental sensitivities\\n* Suitability for mission objectives\\nThe goal is to support AI agents in:\\n* Crop selection optimization\\n* Resource allocation decisions\\n* Nutritional balancing\\n* Stress prioritization\\n---\\n# 3.2 Crop Selection Criteria\\nCrops were selected based on:\\n* High edible biomass fraction (harvest index)\\n* Short to moderate growth cycle\\n* High nutrient density\\n* Compatibility with hydroponic systems\\n* Controlled environment adaptability\\nPrimary crop categories:\\n* Leafy Greens\\n* Root/Tuber Crops\\n* Legumes\\n* Culinary Herbs\\n---\\n# 3.3 Leafy Greens \\u2013 Lettuce (Lactuca sativa)\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Growth cycle: 30\\u201345 days\\n* Harvest index: ~0.7\\u20130.9 (most biomass edible)\\n* Typical yield (CEA systems): 3\\u20135 kg/m\\u00b2 per cycle\\n* Root depth: shallow\\n* Growth habit: vegetative leaf biomass\\nFast turnover makes lettuce suitable for continuous harvest systems.\\n---\\n## Environmental Requirements\\n* Optimal temperature: 15\\u201322\\u00b0C\\n* Heat stress threshold: >25\\u00b0C (risk of bolting)\\n* Optimal relative humidity: 50\\u201370%\\n* Light requirement: 150\\u2013250 \\u00b5mol/m\\u00b2/s PAR\\n* CO\\u2082 enrichment benefit: strong\\n---\\n## Water & Nutrient Requirements\\n* High water demand (high tissue water content ~95%)\\n* Nitrogen-sensitive crop\\n* Requires balanced N-P-K solution\\n* Optimal nutrient solution pH: 5.5\\u20136.5\\nWater use efficiency improves significantly under hydroponics.\\n---\\n## Nutritional Contribution (per 100g fresh weight)\\n* ~15 kcal\\n* Rich in Vitamin K\\n* Good source of Vitamin A (beta-carotene)\\n* Moderate folate content\\n* Low protein (~1.4 g)\\nRole in mission:\\nMicronutrient density and dietary diversity.\\n---\\n## Sensitivity Profile\\nHigh sensitivity:\\n* Heat stress\\n* Water deficit\\n* Nitrogen deficiency\\nModerate sensitivity:\\n* Salinity\\nLow tolerance to environmental fluctuation.\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nAdvantages:\\n* Rapid harvest cycles\\n* High edible biomass fraction\\n* Micronutrient rich\\nLimitations:\\n* Low caloric density\\n* Low protein contribution\\nBest used as:\\nMicronutrient stabilizer crop in multi-crop system.\\n---\\n# 3.4 Potatoes (Solanum tuberosum)\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Growth cycle: 70\\u2013120 days\\n* Harvest index: ~0.75\\n* Typical CEA yield: 4\\u20138 kg/m\\u00b2 per cycle\\n* Growth habit: underground tuber development\\nHigh caloric return per unit area.\\n---\\n## Environmental Requirements\\n* Optimal temperature: 16\\u201320\\u00b0C\\n* Heat stress threshold: >25\\u201328\\u00b0C\\n* Light requirement: 200\\u2013400 \\u00b5mol/m\\u00b2/s\\n* Requires good root-zone aeration\\n---\\n## Water & Nutrient Requirements\\n* Moderate to high water requirement\\n* High potassium demand\\n* Moderate nitrogen demand\\n* Sensitive to waterlogging\\nOptimal nutrient pH: 5.5\\u20136.0\\n---\\n## Nutritional Contribution (per 100g fresh weight)\\n* ~77 kcal\\n* ~17 g carbohydrates\\n* ~2 g protein\\n* Good potassium source\\n* Low fat content\\nRole in mission:\\nPrimary carbohydrate and caloric backbone.\\n---\\n## Sensitivity Profile\\nModerate sensitivity:\\n* Drought\\n* Heat stress\\nHigh sensitivity:\\n* Root hypoxia\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nAdvantages:\\n* High calorie density\\n* High yield per m\\u00b2\\n* Relatively stable storage potential\\nLimitations:\\n* Longer growth cycle\\n* Less micronutrient diversity\\nBest used as:\\nPrimary energy security crop.\\n---\\n# 3.5 Radishes (Raphanus sativus)\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Growth cycle: 21\\u201330 days\\n* Harvest index: ~0.6\\u20130.8\\n* Yield: 2\\u20134 kg/m\\u00b2 per cycle\\n* Compact root vegetable\\nVery fast biomass turnover.\\n---\\n## Environmental Requirements\\n* Optimal temperature: 15\\u201322\\u00b0C\\n* Sensitive to inconsistent watering\\n* Light requirement: moderate\\n---\\n## Water & Nutrient Requirements\\n* Consistent moisture required\\n* Moderate nitrogen demand\\n---\\n## Nutritional Contribution (per 100g fresh weight)\\n* ~16 kcal\\n* Vitamin C source\\n* Moderate fiber content\\nRole in mission:\\nDiet diversity and fast yield buffering.\\n---\\n## Sensitivity Profile\\nHigh sensitivity:\\n* Water inconsistency\\nModerate sensitivity:\\n* Heat stress\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nAdvantages:\\n* Very short cycle\\n* Rapid system feedback crop\\nLimitations:\\n* Low caloric density\\nBest used as:\\nShort-cycle stability and variety crop.\\n---\\n# 3.6 Beans & Peas (Legumes)\\nRepresentative crops:\\n* Common bean (Phaseolus vulgaris)\\n* Pea (Pisum sativum)\\n---\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Growth cycle: 50\\u201370 days\\n* Harvest index: ~0.5\\u20130.6\\n* Yield: 2\\u20134 kg/m\\u00b2\\nModerate cycle duration.\\n---\\n## Environmental Requirements\\n* Optimal temperature: 18\\u201325\\u00b0C\\n* Sensitive to frost\\n* Moderate light requirement\\n---\\n## Water & Nutrient Requirements\\n* Moderate water need\\n* Nitrogen fixation capability (symbiotic bacteria)\\n* Phosphorus-sensitive\\nIn hydroponics, nitrogen fixation may be reduced and external supply required.\\n---\\n## Nutritional Contribution (per 100g fresh weight)\\n* ~80\\u2013120 kcal\\n* 5\\u20139 g protein\\n* Complex carbohydrates\\n* Fiber-rich\\nRole in mission:\\nPrimary plant-based protein source.\\n---\\n## Sensitivity Profile\\nModerate sensitivity:\\n* Salinity\\n* Water stress\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nAdvantages:\\n* Protein supply\\n* Nutritional diversity\\nLimitations:\\n* Moderate yield compared to potatoes\\n* Longer growth than leafy greens\\nBest used as:\\nProtein security crop.\\n---\\n# 3.7 Culinary Herbs (e.g., Basil, Chives)\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Short growth cycle\\n* Low space requirement\\n* Low biomass yield\\n---\\n## Nutritional & Psychological Role\\n* Minimal caloric contribution\\n* Improve palatability\\n* Increase dietary satisfaction\\n* Support crew morale\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nBest used as:\\nPsychological well-being enhancer.\\n---\\n# 3.8 Comparative Crop Matrix\\n| Crop | Cycle Length | Caloric Density | Protein Contribution | Water Demand | System Stability Role |\\n| - | - | - | - | - | - |\\n| Lettuce | Short | Low | Low | High | Micronutrient |\\n| Potato | Long | High | Moderate | Moderate | Energy Backbone |\\n| Radish | Very Short | Low | Low | Moderate | Fast Buffer |\\n| Beans/Peas | Medium | Moderate | High | Moderate | Protein Security |\\n---\\n# 3.9 Strategic Crop Portfolio Logic\\nBalanced mission crop portfolio should:\\n* Ensure caloric security (potatoes)\\n* Ensure protein adequacy (legumes)\\n* Ensure micronutrient sufficiency (leafy greens)\\n* Ensure psychological diversity (herbs)\\n* Maintain short-cycle crop for system resilience (radish/lettuce)\\nAI agents may dynamically adjust planting density and crop ratios based on:\\n* Resource availability\\n* Nutritional deficits\\n* Environmental instability\\n* Energy constraints\\n\", \"score\": 0.42205018, \"location\": {\"s3Location\": {\"uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/03_Crop_Profiles_Extended.md\"}, \"type\": \"S3\"}, \"metadata\": {\"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/03_Crop_Profiles_Extended.md\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-file-modality\": \"TEXT\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-data-source-id\": \"OGUYTEJRIW\"}}], \"total_results\": 3}"}

query: What is the minimum viable diet from greenhouse crops? What nutritional gaps are hardest to fill?

Psychological satisfaction\\nShort-term micronutrient variation is tolerable.\\nSustained caloric or protein deficit is not.\\n---\\n# 5.9 AI-Oriented Nutritional Control Framework\\nAutonomous greenhouse agents may follow:\\n1. Calculate daily nutrient output\\n2. Compare with crew requirements\\n3. Detect deficit or surplus\\n4. Adjust crop ratio or growth parameters\\n5. Monitor correction trajectory\\nExample:\\nIF:\\nCalorie output = 9,500 kcal/day\\nTarget = 12,000 kcal/day\\nGap = 2,500 kcal\\nSystem response:\\n* Increase potato planting density\\n* Increase photoperiod for tuber crops\\n* Reallocate low-calorie crops\\n---\\n# 5.10 Mission-Level Objective Function\\nA simplified optimization goal for agents:\\nMaximize:\\nNutritional Coverage Score\\nSubject to:\\n* Water constraint\\n* Energy constraint\\n* Area constraint\\n* System stability constraint\\nWhere:\\nNutritional Coverage Score =\\nWeighted coverage of calories + protein + micronutrients.\\n\", \"score\": 0.5664378, \"location\": {\"s3Location\": {\"uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/05_Human_Nutritional_Strategy.md\"}, \"type\": \"S3\"}, \"metadata\": {\"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/05_Human_Nutritional_Strategy.md\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-file-modality\": \"TEXT\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-data-source-id\": \"OGUYTEJRIW\"}}, {\"content\": \"Excellent choice.\\n# 3. Extended Crop Profiles for Martian Controlled Environment Agriculture\\n## 3.1 Purpose\\nThis document provides structured agronomic profiles of selected crops suitable for a Martian greenhouse supporting a crew of four astronauts during a 450-day surface mission.\\nEach crop profile includes:\\n* Growth characteristics\\n* Yield potential\\n* Resource requirements\\n* Nutritional contribution\\n* Environmental sensitivities\\n* Suitability for mission objectives\\nThe goal is to support AI agents in:\\n* Crop selection optimization\\n* Resource allocation decisions\\n* Nutritional balancing\\n* Stress prioritization\\n---\\n# 3.2 Crop Selection Criteria\\nCrops were selected based on:\\n* High edible biomass fraction (harvest index)\\n* Short to moderate growth cycle\\n* High nutrient density\\n* Compatibility with hydroponic systems\\n* Controlled environment adaptability\\nPrimary crop categories:\\n* Leafy Greens\\n* Root/Tuber Crops\\n* Legumes\\n* Culinary Herbs\\n---\\n# 3.3 Leafy Greens \\u2013 Lettuce (Lactuca sativa)\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Growth cycle: 30\\u201345 days\\n* Harvest index: ~0.7\\u20130.9 (most biomass edible)\\n* Typical yield (CEA systems): 3\\u20135 kg/m\\u00b2 per cycle\\n* Root depth: shallow\\n* Growth habit: vegetative leaf biomass\\nFast turnover makes lettuce suitable for continuous harvest systems.\\n---\\n## Environmental Requirements\\n* Optimal temperature: 15\\u201322\\u00b0C\\n* Heat stress threshold: >25\\u00b0C (risk of bolting)\\n* Optimal relative humidity: 50\\u201370%\\n* Light requirement: 150\\u2013250 \\u00b5mol/m\\u00b2/s PAR\\n* CO\\u2082 enrichment benefit: strong\\n---\\n## Water & Nutrient Requirements\\n* High water demand (high tissue water content ~95%)\\n* Nitrogen-sensitive crop\\n* Requires balanced N-P-K solution\\n* Optimal nutrient solution pH: 5.5\\u20136.5\\nWater use efficiency improves significantly under hydroponics.\\n---\\n## Nutritional Contribution (per 100g fresh weight)\\n* ~15 kcal\\n* Rich in Vitamin K\\n* Good source of Vitamin A (beta-carotene)\\n* Moderate folate content\\n* Low protein (~1.4 g)\\nRole in mission:\\nMicronutrient density and dietary diversity.\\n---\\n## Sensitivity Profile\\nHigh sensitivity:\\n* Heat stress\\n* Water deficit\\n* Nitrogen deficiency\\nModerate sensitivity:\\n* Salinity\\nLow tolerance to environmental fluctuation.\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nAdvantages:\\n* Rapid harvest cycles\\n* High edible biomass fraction\\n* Micronutrient rich\\nLimitations:\\n* Low caloric density\\n* Low protein contribution\\nBest used as:\\nMicronutrient stabilizer crop in multi-crop system.\\n---\\n# 3.4 Potatoes (Solanum tuberosum)\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Growth cycle: 70\\u2013120 days\\n* Harvest index: ~0.75\\n* Typical CEA yield: 4\\u20138 kg/m\\u00b2 per cycle\\n* Growth habit: underground tuber development\\nHigh caloric return per unit area.\\n---\\n## Environmental Requirements\\n* Optimal temperature: 16\\u201320\\u00b0C\\n* Heat stress threshold: >25\\u201328\\u00b0C\\n* Light requirement: 200\\u2013400 \\u00b5mol/m\\u00b2/s\\n* Requires good root-zone aeration\\n---\\n## Water & Nutrient Requirements\\n* Moderate to high water requirement\\n* High potassium demand\\n* Moderate nitrogen demand\\n* Sensitive to waterlogging\\nOptimal nutrient pH: 5.5\\u20136.0\\n---\\n## Nutritional Contribution (per 100g fresh weight)\\n* ~77 kcal\\n* ~17 g carbohydrates\\n* ~2 g protein\\n* Good potassium source\\n* Low fat content\\nRole in mission:\\nPrimary carbohydrate and caloric backbone.\\n---\\n## Sensitivity Profile\\nModerate sensitivity:\\n* Drought\\n* Heat stress\\nHigh sensitivity:\\n* Root hypoxia\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nAdvantages:\\n* High calorie density\\n* High yield per m\\u00b2\\n* Relatively stable storage potential\\nLimitations:\\n* Longer growth cycle\\n* Less micronutrient diversity\\nBest used as:\\nPrimary energy security crop.\\n---\\n# 3.5 Radishes (Raphanus sativus)\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Growth cycle: 21\\u201330 days\\n* Harvest index: ~0.6\\u20130.8\\n* Yield: 2\\u20134 kg/m\\u00b2 per cycle\\n* Compact root vegetable\\nVery fast biomass turnover.\\n---\\n## Environmental Requirements\\n* Optimal temperature: 15\\u201322\\u00b0C\\n* Sensitive to inconsistent watering\\n* Light requirement: moderate\\n---\\n## Water & Nutrient Requirements\\n* Consistent moisture required\\n* Moderate nitrogen demand\\n---\\n## Nutritional Contribution (per 100g fresh weight)\\n* ~16 kcal\\n* Vitamin C source\\n* Moderate fiber content\\nRole in mission:\\nDiet diversity and fast yield buffering.\\n---\\n## Sensitivity Profile\\nHigh sensitivity:\\n* Water inconsistency\\nModerate sensitivity:\\n* Heat stress\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nAdvantages:\\n* Very short cycle\\n* Rapid system feedback crop\\nLimitations:\\n* Low caloric density\\nBest used as:\\nShort-cycle stability and variety crop.\\n---\\n# 3.6 Beans & Peas (Legumes)\\nRepresentative crops:\\n* Common bean (Phaseolus vulgaris)\\n* Pea (Pisum sativum)\\n---\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Growth cycle: 50\\u201370 days\\n* Harvest index: ~0.5\\u20130.6\\n* Yield: 2\\u20134 kg/m\\u00b2\\nModerate cycle duration.\\n---\\n## Environmental Requirements\\n* Optimal temperature: 18\\u201325\\u00b0C\\n* Sensitive to frost\\n* Moderate light requirement\\n---\\n## Water & Nutrient Requirements\\n* Moderate water need\\n* Nitrogen fixation capability (symbiotic bacteria)\\n* Phosphorus-sensitive\\nIn hydroponics, nitrogen fixation may be reduced and external supply required.\\n---\\n## Nutritional Contribution (per 100g fresh weight)\\n* ~80\\u2013120 kcal\\n* 5\\u20139 g protein\\n* Complex carbohydrates\\n* Fiber-rich\\nRole in mission:\\nPrimary plant-based protein source.\\n---\\n## Sensitivity Profile\\nModerate sensitivity:\\n* Salinity\\n* Water stress\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nAdvantages:\\n* Protein supply\\n* Nutritional diversity\\nLimitations:\\n* Moderate yield compared to potatoes\\n* Longer growth than leafy greens\\nBest used as:\\nProtein security crop.\\n---\\n# 3.7 Culinary Herbs (e.g., Basil, Chives)\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Short growth cycle\\n* Low space requirement\\n* Low biomass yield\\n---\\n## Nutritional & Psychological Role\\n* Minimal caloric contribution\\n* Improve palatability\\n* Increase dietary satisfaction\\n* Support crew morale\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nBest used as:\\nPsychological well-being enhancer.\\n---\\n# 3.8 Comparative Crop Matrix\\n| Crop | Cycle Length | Caloric Density | Protein Contribution | Water Demand | System Stability Role |\\n| - | - | - | - | - | - |\\n| Lettuce | Short | Low | Low | High | Micronutrient |\\n| Potato | Long | High | Moderate | Moderate | Energy Backbone |\\n| Radish | Very Short | Low | Low | Moderate | Fast Buffer |\\n| Beans/Peas | Medium | Moderate | High | Moderate | Protein Security |\\n---\\n# 3.9 Strategic Crop Portfolio Logic\\nBalanced mission crop portfolio should:\\n* Ensure caloric security (potatoes)\\n* Ensure protein adequacy (legumes)\\n* Ensure micronutrient sufficiency (leafy greens)\\n* Ensure psychological diversity (herbs)\\n* Maintain short-cycle crop for system resilience (radish/lettuce)\\nAI agents may dynamically adjust planting density and crop ratios based on:\\n* Resource availability\\n* Nutritional deficits\\n* Environmental instability\\n* Energy constraints\\n\", \"score\": 0.48424238, \"location\": {\"s3Location\": {\"uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/03_Crop_Profiles_Extended.md\"}, \"type\": \"S3\"}, \"metadata\": {\"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/03_Crop_Profiles_Extended.md\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-file-modality\": \"TEXT\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-data-source-id\": \"OGUYTEJRIW\"}}], \"total_results\": 2}"}

query: How do nutritional needs change during high-activity EVA mission days vs regular days?

content.text: {"statusCode":200,"body":"{\"retrieved_chunks\": [{\"content\": \"# 5. Human Nutritional Strategy for a 4-Astronaut Mars Surface Mission\\n## 5.1 Purpose\\nThis document defines the nutritional requirements of a 4-astronaut crew during a 450-day Mars surface mission and establishes a framework for optimizing greenhouse crop production to supplement stored food supplies.\\nThe objective is to:\\n* Maximize nutritional coverage\\n* Maintain physiological health in reduced gravity\\n* Support cognitive performance\\n* Minimize reliance on pre-packaged food\\n* Optimize resource use (water, energy, area)\\nThis framework is designed to support AI agents in making crop allocation and environmental control decisions.\\n---\\n# 5.2 Daily Nutritional Requirements per Astronaut\\nValues represent average adult requirements under moderate physical activity.\\n## 5.2.1 Energy (Calories)\\n* 2,500 \\u2013 3,800 kcal/day per astronaut\\n* Mission planning average assumption: ~3,000 kcal/day\\nFor 4 astronauts:\\n* \\u2248 12,000 kcal/day total\\n* \\u2248 5.4 million kcal over 450 days\\nGreenhouse objective:\\nSupplement a significant fraction of total caloric intake (target variable for optimization).\\n---\\n## 5.2.2 Macronutrient Distribution\\nRecommended macronutrient breakdown:\\n* Carbohydrates: ~45\\u201355% of total calories\\n* Protein: ~15\\u201320% of total calories\\n* Fat: ~30\\u201335% of total calories\\nMars greenhouse is expected to primarily supply:\\n* Carbohydrates\\n* Plant-based protein\\n* Micronutrients\\nFat supply likely partially reliant on stored food unless oil crops are introduced.\\n---\\n## 5.2.3 Protein Requirements\\nRecommended intake:\\n* 1.2\\u20131.8 g protein per kg body mass\\n* Assume 75 kg astronaut average\\nProtein per astronaut:\\n~90\\u2013135 g/day\\nFor 4 astronauts:\\n~360\\u2013540 g protein/day\\nImportance in space:\\n* Muscle mass preservation in 0.38g gravity\\n* Immune function\\n* Tissue repair\\n---\\n## 5.2.4 Micronutrients\\nCritical micronutrients include:\\n* Vitamin A\\n* Vitamin C\\n* Vitamin K\\n* Folate\\n* Iron\\n* Potassium\\n* Magnesium\\nDeficiencies can impact:\\n* Bone health\\n* Immune function\\n* Cognitive performance\\n* Cardiovascular stability\\nLeafy greens are primary micronutrient suppliers.\\n---\\n## 5.2.5 Water\\nMinimum hydration requirement:\\n* 2.1\\u20132.5 L/day per astronaut\\nTotal crew:\\n~8\\u201310 L/day\\nWater for irrigation must be efficiently recycled and managed within the greenhouse system.\\n---\\n# 5.3 Nutritional Contribution by Crop Type\\n## 5.3.1 Caloric Contribution\\n| Crop | Approx kcal per 100g | Role |\\n| - | - | - |\\n| Potato | ~77 kcal | Primary energy |\\n| Beans/Peas | ~80\\u2013120 kcal | Protein + energy |\\n| Lettuce | ~15 kcal | Micronutrients |\\n| Radish | ~16 kcal | Diet diversity |\\nHigh-calorie density crops are necessary for meeting total energy demand.\\n---\\n## 5.3.2 Protein Contribution\\nPrimary greenhouse protein source:\\n* Beans and peas\\nSecondary contribution:\\n* Potatoes (low but additive)\\n* Leafy greens (minor contribution)\\nIf protein deficit occurs, agents should:\\n* Increase legume production\\n* Adjust nutrient solution to support pod development\\n---\\n## 5.3.3 Micronutrient Contribution\\nLeafy greens provide:\\n* Vitamin K\\n* Vitamin A\\n* Folate\\nRadishes contribute:\\n* Vitamin C\\nPotatoes provide:\\n* Potassium\\n* Some Vitamin C\\nBalanced crop diversity prevents micronutrient monotony.\\n---\\n# 5.4 Nutritional Optimization Logic\\nAI agents may implement dynamic nutritional balancing.\\n## 5.4.1 Caloric Deficit Scenario\\nIF:\\n* Total daily caloric production < 12,000 kcal\\nTHEN:\\n* Increase potato allocation\\n* Increase planting density of calorie-dense crops\\n* Adjust light intensity to boost tuber biomass\\n* Temporarily reduce low-calorie crops\\n---\\n## 5.4.2 Protein Deficit Scenario\\nIF:\\n* Daily protein production < 360 g\\nTHEN:\\n* Increase legume cultivation area\\n* Optimize flowering conditions\\n* Adjust nitrogen levels\\n* Extend legume harvest cycle\\n---\\n## 5.4.3 Micronutrient Risk Scenario\\nIF:\\n* Leafy green yield decreases\\n  OR\\n* Micronutrient coverage insufficient\\nTHEN:\\n* Prioritize fast-cycle leafy crops\\n* Increase nitrogen availability\\n* Optimize CO\\u2082 enrichment\\n---\\n# 5.5 Crop Allocation Strategy Model\\nGreenhouse area allocation must balance:\\n* Energy security\\n* Protein adequacy\\n* Dietary diversity\\n* Growth cycle stagger\\nExample strategic allocation model:\\n* 40\\u201350% area: Potatoes\\n* 20\\u201330% area: Legumes\\n* 15\\u201320% area: Leafy greens\\n* 5\\u201310% area: Radishes & herbs\\nAI agents may adjust allocation dynamically based on:\\n* Resource availability\\n* Stress events\\n* Energy constraints\\n* Nutritional deficits\\n---\\n# 5.6 Time-Scale Considerations\\nShort-cycle crops:\\n* Lettuce\\n* Radish\\nAdvantages:\\n* Rapid nutritional correction\\n* System feedback indicator\\nLong-cycle crops:\\n* Potatoes\\nAdvantages:\\n* High caloric return\\n* Energy backbone\\nBalanced portfolio prevents single-point failure.\\n---\\n# 5.7 Sustainability & Closed-Loop Considerations\\nNutritional optimization must integrate:\\n* Water recycling efficiency\\n* Nutrient recovery\\n* Crop residue composting (if applicable)\\n* Waste minimization\\nTrade-off:\\nHigher yield may require higher energy input.\\nAI agents must optimize:\\nYield per unit of energy and water.\\n---\\n# 5.8 Nutritional Risk Hierarchy\\nMission-critical priorities:\\n1. Total caloric sufficiency\\n2. Adequate protein supply\\n3. Micronutrient diversity\\n4. Psychological satisfaction\\nShort-term micronutrient variation is tolerable.\\nSustained caloric or protein deficit is not.\\n---\\n# 5.9 AI-Oriented Nutritional Control Framework\\nAutonomous greenhouse agents may follow:\\n1. Calculate daily nutrient output\\n2. Compare with crew requirements\\n3. Detect deficit or surplus\\n4. Adjust crop ratio or growth parameters\\n5. Monitor correction trajectory\\nExample:\\nIF:\\nCalorie output = 9,500 kcal/day\\nTarget = 12,000 kcal/day\\nGap = 2,500 kcal\\nSystem response:\\n* Increase potato planting density\\n* Increase photoperiod for tuber crops\\n* Reallocate low-calorie crops\\n---\\n# 5.10 Mission-Level Objective Function\\nA simplified optimization goal for agents:\\nMaximize:\\nNutritional Coverage Score\\nSubject to:\\n* Water constraint\\n* Energy constraint\\n* Area constraint\\n* System stability constraint\\nWhere:\\nNutritional Coverage Score =\\nWeighted coverage of calories + protein + micronutrients.\\n\", \"score\": 0.46630657, \"location\": {\"s3Location\": {\"uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/05_Human_Nutritional_Strategy.md\"}, \"type\": \"S3\"}, \"metadata\": {\"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/05_Human_Nutritional_Strategy.md\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-file-modality\": \"TEXT\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-data-source-id\": \"OGUYTEJRIW\"}}, {\"content\": \"Excellent choice.\\n# 3. \"OGUYTEJRIW\"}}, {\"content\": \"drift due to: * System leakage * Consumption imbalance * Sensor issues Plants require CO\\u2082 for photosynthesis but astronauts require safe air composition. --- ## 6.7.2 System Impact Low CO\\u2082: * Reduced photosynthesis High CO\\u2082 (beyond safety limits): * Human health risk Balance is required. --- ## 6.7.3 AI Detection Signals * CO\\u2082 sensor readings * Photosynthetic efficiency * Environmental alarms --- ## 6.7.4 Recommended Responses Low CO\\u2082: * Increase enrichment * Validate sensors High CO\\u2082: * Ventilate system * Verify containment --- ## 6.7.5 Recovery Path Maintain stable operating range with continuous monitoring. --- # 6.8 Operational Prioritization Model AI agents should prioritize: 1. Human safety 2. System stability 3. Crop survival 4. Yield optimization This hierarchy supports mission success. --- # 6.9 General AI Response Principles Autonomous responses should be: \\u2714 Data-driven \\u2714 Proportional \\u2714 Reversible when possible \\u2714 Documented \\u2714 Monitored Uncontrolled actions increase risk. --- # 6.10 Simulation Value for Hackathon Operational scenarios enable students to: * Build resilient agents * Test edge cases * Demonstrate reasoning * Improve system design This aligns with innovation objectives.\", \"score\": 0.3980877, \"location\": {\"s3Location\": {\"uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/06_Greenhouse_Operational_Scenarios.md\"}, \"type\": \"S3\"}, \"metadata\": {\"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/06_Greenhouse_Operational_Scenarios.md\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-file-modality\": \"TEXT\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-chunk-id\": \"1%3A0%3AeJPbpZwBQg_XFYypi8cS\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-data-source-id\": \"OGUYTEJRIW\"}}], \"total_results\": 3}"}
